item.title: Government wants “rational exploration” of maritime areas through the concession of private use
item.description:
The Government yesterday presented a bill that provides for the concession of private use of Macau's maritime areas, with a view to promoting “rational exploration” and avoiding “abandoned areas” of the region's maritime territory. Wong Sio Chak, spokesperson for the Executive Council, assured that the authorities will “actively” protect the marine environment and that sea use projects will be assessed and monitored by those from the Environmental Protection Services, also ensuring the protection of pink dolphins.
In the future, private entities will be able to request the Government to authorize the use of specific maritime areas in Macau, through a private use concession or temporary use license. Holders of the right to use maritime areas will be subject, at the same time, to payment of a fee for the use of maritime areas. These were some of the provisions suggested by the Government in the proposal for a “law on the use of maritime areas”, which was presented yesterday by the Executive Council. According to Wong Sio Chak, spokesperson for the Executive Council, the diploma establishes the conditions for the use and exploitation of maritime areas, which serves to promote “rational exploration” and “sustainable use” of Macau’s maritime territory, with the aim of “avoiding situations of abandoned areas without any use”. The maritime area of ​​the Macao SAR was defined in 2015 by the Central Government, which established an area of ​​85 square kilometers that began to be used and managed by local authorities, with the intention of “promoting the sustainable development of Macau”, said the secretary for Administration and Justice. Susana Wong, the director of the Maritime and Water Affairs Services (DSAMA), revealed that only around a third of Macau's maritime territory is in use. As he explained, the concession for private use of maritime areas is expected to last from 2 to 15 years and will be awarded through a public tender, and only applies to projects for the use of the sea that are of a lasting nature or that involve significant investments, such as projects of public interest or that have an important role in the economic development of Macau. The temporary use license is intended for short-term sea use situations that do not involve significant investments. “The Government reserves greater flexibility [in approving projects] in order to respond to the needs of urban planning”, he highlighted. In relation to activities such as maritime competitions, short-term events or temporary docking of yachts, they are considered “maritime activities” and do not fall within the scope of the law on the use of maritime areas. Susana Wong pointed out that, even if such activities are carried out annually on a regular basis, the organizers simply need to request approval in accordance with the procedures in force within the scope of “maritime activities”.
ECOLOGICAL PROTECTION
Faced with the question of balance between the development of maritime areas and ecological protection, especially the protection of pink dolphins, the Government assumed that it will “actively protect the marine environment and minimize the impacts of projects for the use of maritime areas on the respective environment”. The proposed law also stipulates that all sea use projects must take into account the opinion of the Directorate of Environmental Protection Services (DSPA), reiterated Wong Sio Chak. “Environmental protection constitutes the fundamental principle of this diploma”, assured Susana Wong, indicating that the Functional Maritime Zoning already provides for marine protection areas and reserved maritime zones, in which activities that cause pollution or damage to the environment are prohibited. “When analyzing requests for use of the sea, we will proceed with approval in strict accordance with maritime functional zoning”, he emphasized. For any damage or pollution in maritime areas due to the implementation of private projects, holders will be held responsible for breaking the law and will be subject to fines.
ISLAND FOR CONSTRUCTION WASTE DISPOSAL
The Government's proposal in 2024 to build a landfill zone for construction materials near Hac Sá Beach was widely criticized due to the possible environmental impact on the natural habitat of an endangered Chinese white dolphin species. Several environmental protection associations criticized the project and called for its suspension. A petition opposing the project with more than 1,600 signatures was delivered to the authorities, but the Government, ruling out possible ecological impacts, insists on the need to move forward with the project to solve the problem of urban waste disposal. When referring to the so-called “Ecological Island”, the Executive Council spokesperson highlighted that the project remains in the planning phase and “will be planned according to the Government's concrete situation”. This project is in the nature of “using maritime areas for reasons of public interest”, indicated DSAMA, and therefore will not need a concession attribution for its implementation. “For example, projects promoted by the Public Works Services or DSPA will be subject to consultation through an interdepartmental mechanism, subsequently approved by the Chief Executive, and are not exclusive concessions”, he explained.

item.title: Macau football team defeated by Tanzania in the FIFA Series
item.description:
The Macau football team lost to Tanzania, 6-0, in a match counting for the FIFA Series. It was the second defeat for the team coached by Emmanuel Norway, after the 4-1 defeat against Aruba on Thursday.
The Macau men's football team lost 6-0 to Tanzania on Sunday, in their second game of the FIFA Series, and has not won in official matches for almost seven years. In a game played on Sunday night, in the capital of Rwanda, the African team scored in the 16th minute, with an own goal by Amâncio Goitia, a Benfica Macau player. At Amahoro Stadium, Tanzania scored again in the 26th minute, through defender Bakari Nondo Mwamnyeto, with midfielder Mudathir Yahya scoring the third goal just before the break. The momentum continued into the second half, with striker Paul Peter beating Macau goalkeeper Lei Wa Si for the fourth time, in the 56th minute, before midfielder Novatus Miroshi scored in the 74th minute. Macau had Nuno Pereira in the starting eleven, a player from Portimonense, from the Portuguese II League, who was the only footballer to play abroad among the 25 players called up. The African team – in 112th place in the FIFA rankings – closed the score at 6-0 three minutes from the end of regulation time, through striker Tarryn Allarakhia. The Macau team, coached by Emmanuel Norway, is in 193rd position in the FIFA world rankings, made up of 210 teams. Among the Portuguese-speaking countries or regions, only Timor-Leste is behind Chinese territory, in 198th. This was the 14th official game without a win for Macau, since June 2019, when Filipe Duarte, trained at Benfica, secured the team's first victory in world championship qualifications, beating Sri Lanka 1-0 at home. But the team ended up disqualified, as the Macau Football Association decided not to play the second leg in Sri Lanka, after more than 250 people died in bomb attacks in April 2019. After the pandemic, Macau was one of the last teams to return to the games, in March 2023, after a break of almost four years imposed by the 'zero covid' policy, which included the restriction of entries and quarantines that lasted up to 28 days. Since August 2024, FIFA has banned players without a Macau passport from representing the region. The decision prevented at least five Portuguese players from continuing to play for Macau, including the team's captain, Portuguese-South African Nicholas Torrão, and Filipe Duarste. At the time, a spokeswoman for the Macau Football Association said it would “definitely try again” to reverse the decision, but ruled out the possibility of taking the case to the Court of Arbitration for Sport, in Switzerland.

item.title: Macau university student in intensive care after road accident in Taiwan
item.description: A Macau university student studying in Taiwan was seriously injured after being run over by a bus on campus. The student is currently admitted to the intensive care unit. The car accident occurred last Thursday night. According to the Taiwanese press, at the time of the accident, a bus was traveling along a road on the campus of Chang Gung University, in Taoyuan, and, as it turned a corner, it did not notice the student who was crossing the zebra crossing. The bus ran over the young man, whose lower part of his body was trapped under the vehicle, suffering serious injuries. The 20-year-old student suffered a laceration in the perineal region and several abrasions on his legs, being conscious at the time, and was immediately taken to Chang Gung Hospital to receive medical treatment. Taiwanese authorities said that the bus driver tested negative for alcohol and was transferred to the Taoyuan Prosecutor's Office for investigation. Transport operator SanChung Bus committed to assuming responsibility for all compensation expenses, adding that the team immediately went to the hospital to monitor the situation of the injured person and apologize to the parents and the university. Speaking to Jornal Ou Mun, the Education and Youth Development Services Department (DSEDJ) and the Tourism Services Department (DST) confirmed the incident and have already contacted the student's family through the Macau student association in Taiwan. The authorities, expressing their concern about the case, indicated that they had spoken to the young man's family to find out about the student's current situation. They stated that they continue to maintain close communication with the affected family, providing all necessary assistance.

item.title: Photography competition “Discovering Macau and Hengqin” returns with applications until September
item.description:
The International Institute of Macau (IIM) is promoting another edition of the photography competition “Discovering Macau and Hengqin”, a recurring event that invites amateur and professional photographers to capture the diversity and cultural richness of the two regions. With an image capture period that runs between September 2025 and September 2026, the competition offers monetary prizes, distinctions in two categories and an exhibition.
The International Institute of Macau (IIM) has opened applications for the 2026 edition of the photography competition “Discovering Macau and Hengqin”, an event that has been held annually. The initiative, sponsored by the Macau Foundation, aims to promote the “cultural and heritage diversity of Macau and the In-depth Cooperation Zone between Guangdong and Macau in Hengqin”, aiming to encourage participants to explore natural landscapes, urban settings and the multiple forms of cultural interaction between the two areas. The competition is open to all interested parties aged 18 or over, and is divided into two categories, namely, the digital camera photography category, which uses conventional photographic equipment, and the drone photography category, which allows aerial images to be captured. Each participant can only register in one of the categories and can submit up to five photographs, which must be original and unpublished, not having been previously published or awarded, nor submitted to previous editions of the competition. The images presented must be captured between September 2025 and September 2026, a period that covers more than a year of visual recordings. Works must be delivered in digital JPEG format, with a minimum resolution of two megapixels, and digital montages that cause excessive or unrealistic changes to photographs are not permitted, such as images created with artificial intelligence or altered with software such as Photoshop or Topaz. According to the registration rules, the identification format for each image must follow the structure: photograph number, name of the participant and title of the work. The prizes awarded in each category include a first prize worth five thousand patacas, accompanied by a certificate and trophy for first place. A second prize of three thousand patacas and a third prize of two thousand patacas. Six honorable mentions will also be awarded in each category, worth five hundred patacas each, in addition to a certificate and medal. All participants can request a certificate of participation. The deadline for submitting applications ends on September 25th of the current year. Registration can be made through three different methods, the first option being filling out the online form available on the IIM website. The second option will be to send the documents by email to *protected email*. The third option is to deliver the work on physical media (CD or USB) in a sealed envelope, duly identified, at the IIM headquarters, during office hours. The publication of the results and the awards ceremony are scheduled for the last quarter of 2026. An exhibition will also be organized with a selection of the best works in the competition. The winners will be notified by telephone or email, and attendance at the award ceremony is mandatory, in person or through a representative duly notified to the organization. In addition to IIM, the competition is organized in partnership with the Macau Digital Photography Association (AFDM), the Association of Macau Heritage Ambassadors (MHAA), Halftone – Macau Photographic Association and Macau Cable TV, with the collaboration of the Hong Kong Camera Club, the Association of Young Macanese (AJM) and the Language Exchange & Culture Promotion Association (LECPA).

item.title: Tumors and heart disease remain the biggest causes of death in Macau
item.description:
In 2025, 1,000 people died from tumors in Macau. This is the highest value in at least the last ten years. Next on the list of major causes of death are diseases of the circulatory system and heart. Deaths from diabetes also increased last year.
Tumors and diseases of the circulatory system and heart continue to be the biggest causes of death in Macau. In fact, according to demographic data from the Statistics and Census Services Directorate (DSEC) analyzed by PONTO FINAL, the number of deaths due to tumors last year was the highest in the last ten years, at least. In 2025, the total number of deaths in Macau was 2,424. Of these, 1,000 were due to tumors (neoplasms), and the number of deaths due to malignant tumors was 985 – the highest number in the last decade. Over the last few years, malignant tumors of the bronchi and lungs have been the most common, having caused the death of 262 people last year, for example. In 2024, tumors had killed a total of 944 people, and malignant tumors of the bronchi and lungs were the cause of death for 257 people. In the previous year, 881 people in total had died due to tumors and in 2022 there were 984. Between 2015 and 2021, the number of deaths due to tumors fluctuated between 734 and 884. Diseases of the circulatory system are the second cause of death. Data from DSEC shows that, last year, there were 643 deaths linked to diseases of the circulatory system and a further 398 related to heart diseases. In 2024, diseases of the circulatory system had caused the death of 595 people and heart diseases in another 362. In 2023 and 2022, deaths from circulatory problems reached the peak of the last ten years, with 816 and 879, respectively. Deaths from heart disease in those years were 333 and 267, respectively. The number of deaths due to diseases of the respiratory system has been decreasing in recent years. At the end of 2022, the Government lifted all restrictions related to the pandemic and, similar to what had happened before in the rest of the world, the number of deaths in Macau due to respiratory system problems rose. In 2022 and 2023, the number of deaths from respiratory problems was 415 and 502, respectively. This value has stabilized again in recent years: 371 in 2024 and 163 in 2025. Deaths from diabetes increased last year, reaching the second highest number of deaths in the last decade. Last year, there were 130 deaths due to diabetes, whereas in 2024 only 83 had been recorded. In 2023 was when the number of deaths due to diabetes reached the maximum in the last ten years, with 146 victims. In 2022, there were 127 deaths from diabetes, in 2021 there were 63 and in 2020 only 60. The year 2016 was when there were fewer deaths due to diabetes: 42.

item.title: Japan mulls “decisive measures” with yen at lowest level since 2024
item.description: Tokyo said yesterday that it is considering taking “decisive measures” in the face of the devaluation of the yen, which fell to 160 units per dollar, the lowest level in more than 18 months, due to the rise in oil prices. "We are seeing that speculation is increasing in the foreign exchange market, in addition to the oil futures market. If this situation persists, decisive measures will soon be necessary", declared Japan's Vice Minister of Finance for International Affairs, Atsushi Mimura, quoted by the economic newspaper Nikkei. Japan's top foreign exchange diplomat used, for the first time since taking office in July 2024, the term “decisive”, an expression that traders often interpret as a sign that authorities are willing to intervene. Mimura's statements, which constitute the strongest warning to date about possible intervention, come after the yen reached its lowest level on Friday in New York since July 2024, when the Japanese authorities last intervened. Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda told the House of Representatives Budget Committee that the central bank will continue to “closely monitor” trends in the foreign exchange market, but that, compared to 2025, companies are being more proactive when it comes to increasing wages, which causes exchange rate fluctuations to influence price trends. In this context, the Tokyo Stock Exchange opened sharply lower yesterday, losing more than 4.5% due to concerns about an escalation of the war in the Middle East and possible repercussions on energy supplies, at a time when the Huthi rebels from Yemen have joined the conflict and there are reports that point to a possible land incursion by the United States into Iran.

item.title: Beijing companies interested in investing in Macau in the areas of AI, cybersecurity and biotechnology
item.description: A delegation of more than 20 Macau entrepreneurs in the field of science and technology and biomedicine participated in a conference on artificial intelligence and international expansion of the biomedicine industry in Beijing, on the occasion of the Zhongguancun Forum. At the event, several Beijing companies expressed interest in investing in Macau in the areas of artificial intelligence (AI), cybersecurity and biotechnology, indicated the Trade and Investment Promotion Institute (IPIM). The conference on artificial intelligence and international expansion of the biomedicine industry entitled “Smart Macau: Embarking on a Global Journey” was held in Beijing last Friday. This conference, which took place within the scope of the Zhongguancun Forum, was attended by a delegation of more than 20 Macau businesspeople in the areas of science and technology and biomedicine. IPIM also says that several innovation companies from Beijing have expressed interest in carrying out study visits to Macau and making investments in the territory, covering areas such as artificial intelligence, cybersecurity and biotechnology, among others. The press release details that a company dedicated to artificial intelligence and cybersecurity technologies from Beijing is planning a study visit to Macau to assess the feasibility of establishing R&D or operation bases in Macau; another Beijing biotechnology company to be listed on the stock exchange expressed interest in creating an R&D center and international headquarters in Macau; and a Beijing company dedicated to the research and development of coronary stents expressed its intention to register medical devices in Macau and, through the Macau platform, seek opportunities in foreign markets, particularly in terms of cooperation in the clinical area and regulatory alignment. The conference addressed the “multidimensional synergistic effects of the government, industrial, academic and research and investment sectors”, explains IPIM. More than 20 networking sessions were scheduled at this event, with some participating companies reaching preliminary cooperation intentions with Macau companies, according to IPIM. In this way, “a high-quality platform was provided for companies from Beijing and Macau to boost industrial development and valorization and international expansion, thus contributing to a bright future of science and technology”, reads the press release, which adds that “IPIM received several requests for information from companies in mainland China about their investment intentions in Macau, the consultations covered the sectors of artificial intelligence, ‘big health’, biomedicine, among others, with Chinese companies demonstrating particular interest in Macau’s business environment and investment policies”. Che Weng Keong, president of the Trade and Investment Promotion Institute (IPIM), stated in his speech at the conference that Macau currently has four state reference laboratories and is promoting preparatory work on the construction of the Macau and Hengqin International Education (University) City and the Macau Science and Technology Development Research Industrial Park, in which the model of “synergistic innovation of R&D in Macau + Transformation into Hengqin”, in order to establish a ‘hub’ that should serve for the international expansion of Chinese companies and the implementation of business in Macau and Hengqin by foreign technology companies. Furthermore, the Government Guidance Fund aims to help companies accelerate the transformation of scientific research results and explore new international markets. This conference attracted the participation of more than 130 business and institutional representatives from leading companies and specialized start-ups dedicated to the high and new technology sectors, biomedicine, from Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, among others.

item.title: Taking “The Violin Case” to the world
item.description:
Director Max Bessmertny has been traveling the world, together with co-producer Virginia Ho, to promote his film “The Violin Case” and promote Macau's film industry.
In 2020, while the entire city was facing the challenges of Covid and lockdowns, local filmmaker Max Bessmertny had an idea for a story that would become the basis of his first feature film project, “The Violin Case”. The plot was inspired by a real-life event, when his father, artist Konstantin Bessmertny, accidentally left one of his valuable paintings in the back seat of a taxi in Hong Kong. To this day, the artwork has never been found, but the film it inspired was successfully completed despite the pandemic. In 2021, Max and the project's co-producer, Virginia Ho, began looking for financing for the film and planning pre-production, and in September 2022, they shot the film in Macau, with an almost entirely local cast and crew. After months of editing and post-production, the film was finally presented to an appreciative local audience of friends, supporters and film buffs at Galaxy Macau in 2023. And since that first screening, Max and Virginia have been busy traveling the world and promoting the film at various prestigious film festivals and markets. “2025 was an incredible year, taking the film to different festivals and film markets all over the world”, says Max, speaking to Macau Closer. “We met a lot of interesting people, participated in panels, hosted private screenings and talked to sales agents, decision makers and, of course, other filmmakers.” “What we’ve learned is that the industry is really changing, and the old way of doing things no longer works,” he adds. "We realize that the power to promote the film is in our hands, so we are planning to try to distribute the film ourselves, starting here in Macau and Hong Kong, and then in Southeast Asia and beyond. This means we are venturing a little into the unknown, but as the first film, it will be a very valuable and educational experience to see how it works." While making the film available on streaming services is a long-term goal, initially Max still wants the film to be seen in theaters. "As a filmmaker, I want to have a dialogue with the audience and see how they react, whether positively or negatively. That dialogue is part of what we crave, it gives us the kind of dopamine we need." In total, Max and Virginia traveled to 10 film festivals last year, including HK Filmart, Asian Art Film Awards, Marché du Film (Cannes), FIRST International Film Festival (Xining and Beijing), Shanghai TV Festival, Tokyo International Film Festival Content Market, Golden Rooster Film Market, Torino Film Industry and Singapore International Film Festival's Industry Days. After all their travels, the duo believes it was, without a doubt, a year well spent. “After all this, we received a lot of feedback, had a lot of meetings and gained a lot of knowledge and experience, as well as meeting so many people with fantastic ideas for future co-productions and opportunities", says the director. “It was definitely a success for us in terms of getting our name known and publicizing the film”.
And did you receive positive feedback about the film?
"You know, there was a little bit of both. I think, for the most part, people were excited to see that we were able to make something of value and quality with such a small budget and crew, and during Covid," explains Max. "They were surprised by that and by all the different languages spoken in the film. And, in a way, they were also intrigued by my story: a kid born in Vladivostok goes to Macau, speaks Portuguese, ends up in a Portuguese school, and then goes to a British. It’s a bit confusing!”, he laughs. "What I learned was that feedback is great, but the most important thing is to remember why you're doing this and to take pride in your work. We're so proud of the team of people who worked on this project, aspiring actors and film lovers. I'm still amazed that we were able to pull this off during Covid." Another interesting thing they discovered on their travels is that people know Macau, but they don't know that there is a local film industry here. “So it was great to talk to them about the history of cinema here, about our independent filmmakers and, simply, about the history of the city in general.” "From a cinematic point of view, Asia has something really different and unique, and we shouldn't be ashamed of that. Why are we waiting for Hollywood films to come here? There's no reason to do that anymore. We need to make more of our own films. There are so many stories to tell and people want to hear and see them in the cinema." After a year of promoting “The Violin Case”, there is still work to do to get it shown in more cinemas, but participating in so many film festivals and markets has also encouraged Max to start working on his next project. He can't reveal much at the moment, but the director says it will definitely be set in Asia, and perhaps even Macau. "I'm excited to move on to the next project and explore some different genres. This next one will be based on another script I wrote. Virginia and I will be working together again and we've already found some potential partners. It will be a very different and interesting project, so we're aiming to go into pre-production early next year."
Mark Phillips, Macau Closer

item.title: Air China resumes Beijing-Pyongyang flights after six years of suspension
item.description:
Chinese airline Air China resumed direct passenger flights between Beijing and Pyongyang yesterday, after six years of suspension, following the closure of borders in 2020 due to the Covid-19 pandemic.
Flight CA121 took off at 8:05 am from Beijing International Airport and landed shortly before 11:00 am at Sunan International Airport in the North Korean capital, where it was received by the Chinese ambassador to North Korea, Wang Yajun, and other diplomats from the Asian giant, according to Chinese state news agency Xinhua. Passengers at Beijing Airport were seen queuing to check in their luggage with the airline, journalists from the France-Presse (AFP) news agency noted. Zhao Bin, a Pyongyang-bound passenger, told AFP that the next step for North Korea would likely be to revive tourism for Chinese visitors. North Korea has been much more cautious than China and remains reluctant to issue tourist visas, despite having previously welcomed numerous tourists, mainly Chinese. When the reestablishment of the air link was announced on March 16, the spokesperson for the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Lin Jian, stated that it was “a positive measure to facilitate friendly exchanges between the people of China and North Korea”, countries he described as “friendly neighbors”. According to Air China's reservation systems, the service will initially operate once a week, every Monday. The connection between Beijing and Pyongyang was suspended in January 2020, at the beginning of the Covid-19 pandemic, when North Korea closed its borders and drastically limited international transport. On March 12, international passenger rail service between Beijing and Pyongyang, which had been suspended for more than six years, also resumed. China continues to be North Korea's main trading partner, a country with which it shares a border of more than 1,400 kilometers, in a context marked by international sanctions against Pyongyang and closer ties with Moscow. On Saturday, North Korea's state news agency KCNA said that Kim Jong-un indicated in a message to the Chinese President that Beijing-Pyongyang relations are “reaching new heights”, particularly after the summit held between the two in September. “I am pleased to note that traditional relations between the Democratic People's Republic of Korea [the official name of North Korea] and China are reaching new heights,” wrote the North Korean leader, in a message sent to Xi Jinping on Friday. The two leaders met in September in Beijing, after a military parade to mark the 80th anniversary of the end of the Second World War in the Pacific, in which the North Korean sat to the left of Xi, who had the President of Russia, Vladimir Putin, to the right. Lusa

item.title: Hong Kong arrests 42 people in operation against mafias infiltrated in real estate construction
item.description:
The Hong Kong anti-corruption authority and police yesterday announced the arrest of 42 people on suspicion of infiltration by criminal organizations in residential building maintenance projects.
The operation took place on March 26 and 27, following an investigation initiated based on complaints from citizens, received since mid-2025, which pointed to a project consultancy company for facilitating awards through bribes, according to a statement from the Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC). Investigations revealed that the consultant had obtained consultancy contracts with “unreasonably low” fees and, subsequently, had used intermediaries linked to Chinese mafia groups, known as triads, to manipulate the tender processes through corrupt means. Triads emerged between 1842 and 1930, when members of secret societies from China emigrated to Hong Kong and formed mutual aid organizations. One of the cases focuses on a major renovation project for a development in the New Territories, with an estimated value of more than 160 million Hong Kong dollars. The consultant obtained the consultancy contract for a price “much below” reasonable, estimated by ICAC at 0.5% of the total. “Although the competition in question is still in the preparatory phase, it is believed that ICAC’s early intervention prevented the corrupt practices from taking place and prevented the triad from infiltrating the major neighborhood renewal project,” according to the ICAC statement. In another process it is stated that the consultant obtained, in 2022, the consultancy contract for the complete renovation of another building on the island. The project was later awarded 20 million Hong Kong dollars to a contractor, with several million classified as doubtful expenditure. Authorities detained 10 men, including the owner and a registered inspector of the consultancy, as well as intermediaries with a history of links to the triads. In addition, another 32 people were arrested in parallel raids. The intervention comes at the height of an investigation opened following the devastating fire on November 26 at the Wang Fuk Court residential complex, which claimed the lives of 168 people. The police detained 22 people on suspicion of voluntary homicide, in addition to another six on suspicion of fraud, all linked to the fire. The ICAC also detained another 23 people, including consultants, contractors and members of the complex's condominium association, on suspicion of involuntary homicide, serious negligence and corruption, including possible manipulation of proposals and the use of non-fireproof materials. The tragedy has triggered intense scrutiny over practices in the building maintenance sector, where complaints have mounted about opaque tenders, excessive costs and risks to the safety of neighbors. Lusa

item.title: “We are not monsters”, the postponed identity of trans people in Macau
item.description:
Four trans people in Macau, where gender identity is not legislated, narrate a path of exclusion, with barriers in access to medical care, employment and education, in which Portuguese nationality can be “a kind of affirmation”.
A-wai only feels good at home. It took him weeks to agree to give this interview and he asks that, after it is published, the audios be deleted. He doesn't want any photos, he makes sure, nor does he want a reference to his Christian name, which, for now, remains engraved on his identity document. He has social anxiety disorder, he says. The first question he asks when he arrives at the designated place is whether he looks like a woman. Later, you will also want to know if you have a female voice. He was born in the 80s, but only recently moved towards sexual reassignment, a desire that was delayed by his family. “Not everyone went to university in 2005, it was an honor for me and my family to do so, so I gave up on myself, tried to live as others wanted”, he tells Lusa. Without the possibility of moving forward, at first, with hormone replacement therapy (HRT) in Macau – one of the initial steps of the transition – he started taking hormones illegally in Thailand. Common practice among the local trans community, including minors, who turn to the black market. “It’s a concern and we don’t want them to do it”, reacts Kelvin Lei, member of the GDC Association (Gender-Diverse Community). “We finally learned that one or two doctors in Macau prescribe HRT (…). A formal medical mechanism needs to be established so that one can receive treatment,” he says. For sex reassignment surgery, A-wai returned to Thailand. In appearance, the change materialized. Not on paper. In Macau there is no gender identity law and it is not permitted to change sex on official documents. A-wai wrote to the Government and parliament. Silence. Anxiety ended up compromising post-operative care, the vagina began to atrophy. “My priority is the identity card”, says the unemployed young woman. To Lusa, the Directorate of Identification Services said that it had received eight written requests to change the sex on the identification document by the end of January, all of which were “rejected, in accordance with the law”. “Since there is still no general consensus in society, the Government (…) has no plans to change the current legal regime”, reads the response. Rejection has other faces: from her family, A-wai received no support, in job interviews she was rejected several times and in the hospital she was sent to the men's ward. The scrutiny is permanent and A-wai admits that he thought about suicide. "One day, a social worker asked me: why isn't it good to be a man? Isn't it good for finding a job?" Bryson (not his real name), a 20-year-old trans man, has the support of his family, but his future is postponed. He dropped out of university after the educational institution – where in the first year it is mandatory to live on campus – did not allow him to stay in the men's dormitory. “The proposed solution did not respect me”, he says. In her earliest memories, Bryson already expressed the anguish of being a girl. He reports a journey of rejection – such as when a doctor asked if he had been sexually harassed for “thinking this way” -, but marked by moments of hope – he was allowed to wear a sports uniform at school instead of a female uniform. At the age of 15, he was referred by the school psychologist, to whom he confided that he had tried to kill himself, to a psychiatrist at the hospital, where he began taking medication to “control his emotions”. “The doctor refused to write gender dysphoria in the files.” Bryson also took HRT even less and without clinical supervision, until he began to be monitored by a doctor in Hong Kong and then Macau. In the case of the young man, who had a mastectomy in Shanghai, there is the possibility of acquiring a Portuguese passport, through his father, and finally seeing an M on a document. “It means that there is a place that accepts my situation and a document that certifies that I am a man”, he notes. But enthusiasm is moderate. A symbolic victory, with little day-to-day functionality. Lusa asked the Portuguese Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MNE) how many people from Macau changed their gender on their citizenship card, but “the Portuguese consular network does not have disaggregated data on requests” at consular posts. “These situations will still be very residual”, indicated the MNE. Jonathan Lai, trans man, 26 years old, has a Portuguese passport. It was with this document that she married her husband in the United States, also from Macau, something that could have happened in Chinese territory. Despite homosexual marriage being prohibited, Macau's identity card is still a woman. At the age of 23, Jonathan had a mastectomy in Thailand and at 24 he had his female organs removed in Taiwan, where he is due to undergo a phalloplasty (construction of a penis) this year. Here too, the beginning of this story is repeated: depression, attempted suicide, first contact with illegal HRT: “An injection cost 91 yuan in China”. Lusa questioned the Macau Health Services about whether they are aware of this reality, but received no response. If “a patient is suspected of having gender dysphoria, a clinical assessment process is initiated and symptomatic treatment is provided.” If the “evaluation indicates the need for specialized treatment, the patient will be referred to a specialist”, was the response. In cases “involving multidisciplinary issues or more complex conditions”, the public hospital “will evaluate the effectiveness of treatment from multiple perspectives, adjusting treatment plans as necessary”, the services added. “The average Macau resident must know that there are trans people among humans and that we are not monsters.” The words are from Clio, a trans woman, almost 40. She dresses as a woman – “my identity” – for the interview, but does not do so when working. It’s called a “survival technique”, he says. One day, dressed as a woman, she passed family members on the street, who acted as if they didn't know each other. They never talked about it. "If I openly say that I am trans, it will affect their lives. They will feel ashamed or lose face", notes the founder of the Macau Transgender Mutual Support Community group (in a free translation), who also asks to hide her identity. Clio was diagnosed with gender dysphoria at the public hospital. “Some time later, I went back to the doctor and asked for HRT and he, reluctantly, prescribed it,” he says. “The more people are diagnosed, the more attention is paid to the issue.” Also for this woman there is a window that will soon open. This week he will deal with the Portuguese citizen card. “It’s a kind of affirmation, a proof”, he explains.
